result:
# Static

The radio in my grandmother's kitchen picked up everything except the station she wanted.

She'd stand there in the mornings, one hand turning the dial with the patience of someone who had learned patience the hard way, and the stations would slide past like towns seen from a train window. Fragments of a weather report. Three seconds of a song she almost recognized. A preacher finishing a sentence that had lost its beginning somewhere over the mountains.

She never got frustrated. That's the part I keep coming back to.

I would sit at the table eating cereal that had gone soft in the milk, watching her, and I thought she was failing at something. I thought: there is a station she wants and she is not finding it. This is a small, quiet failure happening before breakfast. I was nine. I categorized the world into successes and failures the way you do when you are nine and have not yet been humbled into something more nuanced.

But she wasn't failing. She was listening.

The static itself, she told me once, was interesting if you paid attention. She said you could hear the shape of the interference. Weather moving in from the coast. Electrical lines running parallel to the road. Sometimes, she said, late at night, you could hear signals bouncing off the ionosphere from stations hundreds of miles away, voices arriving from places they had no business arriving from. A baseball game from a city she'd never visited. A woman laughing at something her co-host said at two in the morning in a studio she would never see.

I didn't understand what she was telling me. I filed it away in the folder I kept for things adults said that seemed important but made no immediate sense, a folder that has been expanding for thirty years now and shows no sign of closing.

---

I think about her when the noise gets loud.

Not the radio noise. The other kind. The noise that lives in devices now, the noise that is less static and more signal — too much signal, signal without end, a river that has flooded its banks and is now just a flat brown lake covering everything, and you have forgotten where the river was, where the banks were, where the dry ground begins.

She is gone now. The kitchen is gone too, sold, probably a different color, probably housing a different family's mornings. The radio is somewhere. In a box. Or gone. Things go.

What I find myself grieving — not every day, not dramatically, just sometimes, in the way you grieve small disappearances — is not her exactly. I mean her, yes, of course her, that grief is its own continent. But also: the quality of attention she had. The willingness to stand at a counter and turn a dial slowly and be interested in what arrived, even if it was not what she asked for. Especially if it was not what she asked for.

---

I have been trying to write this essay, or story, or whatever it is, for a long time.

Every time I sit down I think I know what it's about and then it becomes about something else. I thought it was about grief. Then I thought it was about attention. Now I think it might be about the difference between looking for something and being open to what's there, and whether those two things can happen at the same time, and whether I have ever actually managed both simultaneously, and the honest answer is: I'm not sure.

There is a version of me that is always tuning. Always arriving at the place just after the song ended, or leaving just before it begins. A version that mistakes motion for searching, and mistakes searching for finding, and stands in the rubbed-out middle place wondering why nothing ever resolves into clarity.

My grandmother had clarity. Or no — that's not right either. She had something better than clarity. She had the ability to be interested in the unclear. To hold the unresolved thing in her hands and turn it over and say: look at this. Look at the texture of this. You don't need to know what it is to notice that it's interesting.

---

The cereal would go completely soft if I waited long enough. I discovered this through a period of experimentation I remember as peaceful. The kitchen smelling of coffee. Light coming through the window above the sink at an angle that meant it was still morning, still early, still the part of the day that hadn't decided anything yet.

She'd find a station eventually, or she wouldn't. Either way she'd pour her coffee and sit across from me and we'd talk about nothing important, which was everything important, which is the thing about being nine and sitting in a kitchen with someone who loves you in the uncomplicated way that some people are capable of loving — you don't know that you're storing it. You don't know that you're building an archive. You think you're just eating cereal.

The static meant something was out there.

All those miles of air, all that invisible electrical weather, and somewhere inside it, if you were patient, if you were genuinely curious rather than just hopeful, something would come through.

She taught me that without meaning to.

I'm still not sure I've learned it.

But I'm still tuning.
